title: Code & Data
text: Every example in the book is built with real, runnable R code. The complete, chapter-by-chapter code and the datasets that go with it are available to readers of the book.

title: Code & Data
text:
A taste of the code
Here is a short example of the kind of code you will write. It reads a CSV file of measurements and makes a simple plot.

# Read a CSV of climate measurements
climate <- read.csv("climate.csv")

# Look at the first few rows
head(climate)

# Plot temperature over time
plot(climate$year, climate$temperature,
     type = "l",
     xlab = "Year",
     ylab = "Temperature",
     main = "Temperature over time")

In the book, examples like this grow step by step into full Earth-data analyses, with each line explained for beginners.

title: Code & Data
text:
Get the full code
The complete, chapter-by-chapter code and datasets are available to readers of the book by request. Here is how it works:

Request access by email with your name, GitHub username, and proof of purchase (an order or receipt number). Request code access
We verify the request and grant you access to a private GitHub repository with all of the code and data.
You clone or download the repository whenever you need it.

A free GitHub account is needed to receive access to the private repository. Readers without a GitHub account can email marsellos@gmail.com and we will send the code as a zip file instead.

title: Free Sample
text:
Want to try the book before you buy it? The free sample includes the full front matter plus Chapter 1, “Introduction to R” (about 60 pages). It is the same content and layout as the printed book, so you can see exactly how it teaches R from the very beginning.
Chapter 1 walks you through installing R, running your first commands, and understanding how the language works, with no prior experience assumed.

Download the free sample (PDF)

title: Datasets
text:
Every analysis in this book uses real, public Earth and environmental data. This page keeps a copy of each dataset, organized by chapter, so you always have a way to get it.
A column guide (a “data dictionary”) follows each dataset: what every column means, the units, and what was actually measured, in everyday language.

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 2 — Preparing trustworthy data

Groundwater levels (USGS)
How high the water sits underground at one monitoring well on Long Island, recorded almost every day for 25 years. Picture a drinking straw pushed deep into the ground: this is the water height inside that straw, tracked over time.
Download usgs_groundwater_…rdb

Source: U.S. Geological Survey, National Water Information System (NWIS). Well 404416073491801, 2000–2025.
Terms: Public domain (U.S. Government work). Free to use and share.
Please credit: “U.S. Geological Survey, National Water Information System.”

Columns








Column
Meaning
Units




agency_cd
Who recorded it (always “USGS”)
text


site_no
The well’s ID number
text


datetime
The day of the reading
date


…_62610_00003
Water level: height of the underground water surface, measured against a fixed nationwide sea-level benchmark (like a “zero” line painted on every well in the country)
feet above the NGVD 1929 datum


…_62610_00003_cd
A quality stamp: A = approved, P = provisional (not yet double-checked)
code





Daily precipitation, JFK Airport (NOAA GHCN-Daily)
How much rain and snow fell each day at New York’s JFK Airport weather station. The book pairs this with the groundwater data to see whether wet periods line up with the water rising.
Download ghcn-daily_USW00094789_JFK.csv

Source: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information, Global Historical Climatology Network – Daily. Station USW00094789 (JFK International Airport).
Terms: Public domain (NOAA / U.S. Government, U.S. station). Free to use and share.
Please credit: “NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information, GHCN-Daily.”

Key columns (the file also carries many extra weather variables)








Column
Meaning
Units




STATION, NAME
The station’s ID and name
text


DATE
The day
date


LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, ELEVATION
Where the station sits
degrees, meters


PRCP
Rain/melted-snow that day. Stored in tenths of a millimeter, so 15 means 1.5 mm — like measuring rainfall with an extra-fine ruler
tenths of mm


TMAX, TMIN
Warmest and coldest temperature that day, also in tenths (so 233 = 23.3 °C)
tenths of °C


SNOW, SNWD
New snowfall and snow depth on the ground
mm


*_ATTRIBUTES
Quality and source flags for the value next to them
codes

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 3 — Spatial structure

Volcano database (Smithsonian, via plotly) — link only
A worldwide list of volcanoes with their locations, heights, and types. The book uses it to plot volcanoes on a map.
We link to this one rather than re-host it: the data comes from the Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program, whose terms allow educational use with credit but not commercial redistribution, so the safest path is to fetch it from the original location.

Get it from: plotly/datasets/volcano_db.csv
Original source / please credit: Smithsonian Institution, Global Volcanism Program — https://volcano.si.edu
Terms: Smithsonian Terms of Use (educational/non-commercial, with citation).

Columns: Volcano Name, Country, Region, Latitude/Longitude (where it is, in degrees), Elev (summit height in meters), Type (e.g. shield, stratovolcano), Status, Last Known (most recent known eruption).


Long Island counties (NYS GIS)
The map outlines of the four Long Island counties — Kings, Queens, Nassau, and Suffolk — used as the base map for the elevation and flood examples. Like tracing the coastline of the island onto paper.
Download nys_long_island_counties.geojson

Source: New York State GIS Program Office, NYS Civil Boundaries (counties layer).
Terms: Public New York State government data. Provided as-is, for planning and general use only, with no warranty (no formal open-data license is published, so we mirror it with credit).
Please credit: “New York State GIS Program Office, NYS Civil Boundaries.”

Columns: NAME (county name), ABBREV (short code), plus the geometry (the actual boundary shape).


Elevation & bathymetry (elevatr / AWS Terrain Tiles) — reproduce on demand
The height of the land (and depth of the nearby seabed) across the region, used to build the digital elevation models, hillshades, and flood maps. Imagine the land covered by a fine checkerboard where every square stores the ground height there — the same idea as the depth numbers printed on the floor of a swimming pool.
This one is assembled on demand from open map tiles rather than stored as a single file. To recreate it, the book calls elevatr::get_elev_raster() for the area and detail level shown in the chapter.

Source: AWS Terrain Tiles (built from public elevation data such as SRTM and the USGS National Elevation Dataset), retrieved via the elevatr R package.
Terms: Open data; credit the underlying providers.
Please credit: “Elevation from AWS Terrain Tiles via the elevatr R package.”
Values: ground (or seabed) height in meters; below sea level is negative.

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 5 — Modeling and prediction

Nottingham temperatures (nottem)
Twenty years of monthly average air temperature at Nottingham Castle, England (1920–1939). A long, steady heartbeat of the seasons, used to practice spotting yearly cycles.
Download nottem.csv

Source: Built into R (the datasets package). Terms: free to use and share (ships with R).

Columns: year, month (Jan–Dec), temp_F (that month’s average temperature, in °F).


New York air quality (airquality)
Daily air-quality and weather readings in New York, May–September 1973. The book uses it to predict ozone from temperature and other factors.
Download airquality.csv

Source: Built into R (the datasets package). Terms: free to use and share.

Columns








Column
Meaning
Units




Ozone
Ozone in the air. Parts per billion is like counting how many red marbles are in a bag of a billion marbles
parts per billion


Solar.R
Strength of sunshine reaching the ground
Langleys


Wind
Wind speed
miles per hour


Temp
Air temperature
°F


Month, Day
When (month 5–9, day 1–31)
number

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 1 — Getting started

Volcano elevation grid (volcano)
A height map of Maungawhau (Mt Eden), a volcano in Auckland, New Zealand, used in the very first plotting examples. The whole hill is laid out as a grid; each cell holds the ground height of a 10 m × 10 m patch — a height map, like a topographic model of the hill.
Download volcano.csv

Source: Built into R (the datasets package). Terms: free to use and share.
Layout: 87 rows × 61 columns of numbers; each number is the elevation in meters at that spot. There are no labels — position in the grid is the location.

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 6 — Case studies

GPS station NYCI (Nevada Geodetic Lab)
Daily position of a GPS ground marker near New York City. Every day it records how far the marker has shifted east, north, and up since a fixed starting point — like marking how far a parked car has slowly rolled from its original spot, measured to the millimeter.
Download gps_NYCI.tenv3

Source: Nevada Geodetic Laboratory, University of Nevada, Reno — station NYCI.
Terms: Open data; please credit. Cite Blewitt, Hammond & Kreemer (2018), Eos, doi:10.1029/2018EO104623.
Please credit: “Nevada Geodetic Laboratory, University of Nevada, Reno.”

Key columns








Column
Meaning
Units




site
Station name (NYCI)
text


YYMMMDD, yyyy.yyyy
The date, written two ways (calendar, and as a decimal year like 2006.31)
date / years


__MJD
A running day-counter scientists use, so any date becomes one plain number (handy for math)
day number


__east(m), _north(m), ____up(m)
How far the marker has moved sideways (east, north) and vertically (up) from its reference position
meters


sig_e(m), sig_n(m), sig_u(m)
The “give or take” — how uncertain each position is
meters


_latitude(deg), _longitude(deg), __height(m)
Where the station is on Earth
degrees, meters





GRACE water-mass change (NASA/JPL) — link to Zenodo
Monthly maps of how much water (including ice) a region has gained or lost, measured by twin satellites that sense tiny changes in Earth’s gravity. The book uses it to watch ice loss over Greenland.
This file is large and is already archived by us, so we link to it:

Get it from: Zenodo record, doi:10.5281/zenodo.20535360
Original source / please credit: NASA/JPL GRACE & GRACE-FO mascons, PO.DAAC, doi:10.5067/TEMSC-3JC634.
Terms: Public domain (NASA / U.S. Government).
Main value: water-mass change expressed as liquid water equivalent thickness (centimeters). That means: if the gained or lost water were spread as an even layer over the area, this is how thick that layer would be — a way to turn “how much water” into a single, easy-to-picture height.



Long Island counties (NYS GIS)
Same county outlines as Chapter 3, reused as the base map for the flood case study. Download nys_long_island_counties.geojson — source, terms, and columns are identical to the Chapter 3 entry.

title: Datasets
text:
Chapter 7 — Global change and sustainability

Greenhouse-gas emissions & population (World Bank, WDI)
For nearly every country, total greenhouse-gas emissions and total population, year by year since 1960. The book divides one by the other to get emissions per person, then maps how that has changed worldwide.
Download emissions EN.GHG.ALL.MT.CE.AR5 (zip) · Download population SP.POP.TOTL (zip)

Source: World Bank, World Development Indicators (WDI).
Terms: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) — free to use and share with credit.
Please credit: “World Bank, World Development Indicators (CC BY 4.0).”

Columns (each file is a wide table)








Column
Meaning
Units




Country Name, Country Code
The country and its 3-letter code (e.g. USA, BRA, KEN)
text


Indicator Name, Indicator Code
What is being measured
text


1960 … 2024
One column per year, holding that year’s value
see below



The emissions value is total greenhouse gases in megatonnes of CO₂-equivalent. Different gases trap heat by different amounts, so “CO₂-equivalent” rebalances them onto one common scale — like converting several currencies into dollars so you can add them into a single total. The population value is simply the number of people.


Country boundaries (Natural Earth)
The outline shapes of every country in the world, used as the canvas for the global emissions maps. A blank world map waiting to be colored in.
Download ne_50m_admin_0_countries.zip

Source: Natural Earth (1:50m cultural vectors), via the rnaturalearth R package.
Terms: Public domain — free to use and share, no restrictions.
Please credit (optional): “Made with Natural Earth.”

Key columns: NAME (country name), ISO_A3 (3-letter country code, used to match the World Bank data), plus the geometry (each country’s outline). The file also carries extras like continent, region, and an estimated population.







Credits and terms, in one place



The datasets above belong to their original providers and are shared here under the terms listed with each. Please keep the credit lines if you reuse them. The book and this site only mirror the public copies for convenience and reliability; they make no claim of ownership over the underlying data.

title: About & Resources
text:
Antonios E. Marsellos, Ph.D. teaches and researches in the Department of Geology, Environment, and Sustainability, Hofstra University.
Katerina G. Tsakiri, Ph.D. teaches and researches in the Department of Information Systems, Analytics and Supply Chain Management, Rider University.

title: About & Resources
text:
Antonios E. Marsellos, Ph.D. teaches and researches in the Department of Geology, Environment, and Sustainability, Hofstra University.
Katerina G. Tsakiri, Ph.D. teaches and researches in the Department of Information Systems, Analytics and Supply Chain Management, Rider University.

title: About & Resources
text:
Our approach
Between us we have spent many years teaching R to students in Earth science, sustainability, and business, including many who arrived with no coding experience at all, from high schoolers to graduate researchers. That experience taught us how to introduce R to true beginners: keep it hands-on, show real results early, and build confidence before theory. This book is the result. It is written to break the ice of coding for people who never thought they could code. The style may not be what experienced R users expect, but for beginners it is very effective.
A guiding belief runs through all of it: students do not struggle with complexity, they struggle with abstraction. With the right approach, anyone can learn to think analytically, work with real data, and contribute to research, regardless of where they start.

title: About & Resources
text:
Imprint
Published by Earth Data Analytics Press (2026).

title: The Book
text: From GPS motion and Greenland’s vanishing ice to coastal flooding and global greenhouse-gas emissions, this book teaches you to analyze the data of a changing planet, using R, starting from zero.

title: The Book
text:
About the book
R for Earth and Sustainability Data Analytics teaches you to turn real environmental data into insight, using R, even if you have never written a line of code. Most R, GIS, and data-science books assume you already program. This one does not. It starts with installing R and builds, step by step, to complete Earth-data case studies: tracking ground motion from GPS stations, measuring Greenland’s ice loss, mapping coastal flood risk, and charting global greenhouse-gas emissions.
Along the way you learn the full modern toolkit: data cleaning, spatial analysis and mapping (GIS), time series, statistical modeling, and an introduction to machine learning, all applied to the data of a changing planet. Every method is taught hands-on with datasets you can download and run yourself, so you finish able to do your own analysis, not just read about it.

title: The Book
text:
What you will learn
The book is organized into seven chapters that build on each other, from the very first line of R code to full Earth-data case studies.

Introduction to R. Install R, run your first commands, and learn how the language works.
Data Handling and Preparation. Read, clean, and reshape data so it is ready to analyze.
Spatial Analysis (GIS). Work with maps, coordinates, and spatial layers in R.
Time Series Fundamentals. Explore data that changes over time and find patterns in it.
Modeling, Prediction, and Machine Learning. Build models that explain and forecast Earth data.
Earth System Case Studies. Apply your skills to real measurements of the planet.
Global Change and Sustainability. Analyze global change data and connect it to sustainability questions.

title: The Book
section: Real data you will work with
text:
Real data you will work with
You work with real data from day one. Every technique is taught with actual Earth and environmental datasets:

GPS station ground displacement
GRACE / Greenland ice-mass change
Coastal flooding and DEM (Long Island, NY)
Global greenhouse-gas emissions (World Bank WDI)
Groundwater levels
Climate and temperature series

title: The Book
text:
Why this book is different
Most spatial and data-science books in R assume you already know how to program and how to do statistics, which leaves beginners in the Earth and environmental sciences to bridge that gap on their own. This book closes it. It is a true on-ramp that takes you from your first command to research-ready analysis, using the real data of a changing planet.
Across many years of teaching R in Earth science, sustainability, and business, we kept seeing the same thing: students do not struggle with complexity, they struggle with abstraction. When an idea stays theoretical it slips away; when it becomes hands-on, it sticks. So this book is built to break the ice of coding, putting real data and real results in front of you from the first session.
This is a deliberate choice. The style may not match what seasoned R users expect, but for a genuine beginner it is exactly what makes coding finally click. The aim is not to memorize commands; it is to learn how to ask better questions of data, and to gain the confidence to keep asking them.

title: The Book
text:
Authors
Antonios E. Marsellos, Ph.D. (Department of Geology, Environment, and Sustainability, Hofstra University).
Katerina G. Tsakiri, Ph.D. (Department of Information Systems, Analytics and Supply Chain Management, Rider University).

title: R for Earth and Sustainability Data Analytics
text:
From GPS motion and Greenland’s vanishing ice to coastal flooding and global greenhouse-gas emissions, this book teaches you to analyze the data of a changing planet, using R, starting from zero.
Learn to analyze real Earth and environmental data from scratch in R: no prior coding or statistics experience required.
This book is built to break the ice of coding for complete beginners. You start by producing real results, and your confidence grows from there.

title: R for Earth and Sustainability Data Analytics
text:
Who this is for
This book is written for students and researchers in environmental science, geology, sustainability, and GIS who are new to R. You do not need any prior programming or statistics background. Each chapter starts with the basics and builds up to working with real Earth data.

title: R for Earth and Sustainability Data Analytics
text:
What makes this book different

Start from zero. No prior coding or statistics needed. You begin by installing R and writing your first command.
Real Earth data from day one. Every method is taught on actual datasets: GPS ground motion, Greenland ice loss, coastal flooding, and global emissions.
The whole modern toolkit. Data cleaning, GIS and mapping, time series, statistical modeling, and an introduction to machine learning, all in one beginner-friendly path.
Reproducible and reusable. Download the code and data, run it yourself, and adapt it to your own research.

title: R for Earth and Sustainability Data Analytics
section: Built for people who have never coded
text:
Built for people who have never coded
We have spent many years teaching R to students in Earth science, sustainability, and business who had never written a line of code, from high schoolers to graduate researchers. That experience shaped every page. We learned a simple lesson: beginners do not struggle with complexity, they struggle with abstraction. So this book keeps everything hands-on and shows real results from the very first session.
This approach is deliberate, and it may not look like a traditional R course. To an experienced programmer it can feel unusual. To a true beginner, it is what finally makes coding click. Seeing a real map or a real trend appear on screen early changes everything: it turns “I could never code” into “let me try the next one.”

Learn R · Explore Earth
